## Intellectual Influences

The tools we use matter, but so do the ideas that shape how we think. The people below have influenced my approach to statistics, machine learning, decision-making, data systems, and scientific computing. While they come from different fields, they share a common emphasis on rigor, curiosity, reproducibility, and practical impact.

### Scientific Machine Learning & Software Craftsmanship

[Vincent Warmerdam](https://koaning.io/) Pragmatic machine learning, MLOps, software engineering practices, and critical thinking about AI.

[Gaël Varoquaux](https://gael-varoquaux.info/) Scientific computing, machine learning, and the development of open-source software for data science.

[Eric Ma](https://ericmjl.github.io/) Scientific computing, network science, reproducible research, and the Python data ecosystem.

### Statistical Modeling & Inference

[Frank Harrell](https://www.fharrell.com/about) Biostatistics, predictive modeling, uncertainty quantification, and evidence-based decision making.

[Michael I. Jordan](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jordan/) Statistical machine learning, artificial intelligence, and the foundations of decision-making systems.

### Decision Science & Reinforcement Learning

[Warren Powell](https://castle.princeton.edu/)  Sequential decision-making, stochastic optimization, reinforcement learning, and operations research.

### Bayesian Statistics & Applied Machine Learning

[Richard McElreath](https://www.eva.mpg.de/ecology/staff/richard-mcelreath/) Bayesian statistics, evolutionary anthropology, and the application of probabilistic models to social science.

[Juan Camilo Orduz](https://juanitorduz.github.io/about/) Bayesian modeling, probabilistic machine learning, experimentation, and modern data science workflows.

### Graph Data & Knowledge Systems

[Tomaz Bratanic](https://github.com/tomasonjo) Knowledge graphs, graph databases, GraphRAG, and the integration of structured knowledge into AI systems.
